response_text:
:::writing
I remember the smell first: coffee that had been sitting on a burner too long, machine oil, and the faint dust of old concrete. It was still dark when I walked into the shop for my first day. The lights buzzed overhead like they were waking up reluctantly. I had a brand-new pair of work boots that squeaked when I walked, which felt like announcing to everyone that I didn’t belong yet.

I had worked before. Plenty of jobs. Grocery store stockroom, a landscaping crew that paid cash on Fridays, a warehouse where nobody knew anyone’s last name. Those jobs had taught me something simple: show up, keep your head down, don’t cause problems, and maybe you’ll keep getting hours.

So that morning I expected more of the same.

A big guy named Raul was the first person to talk to me. He had a gray beard tucked into his collar and a union patch sewn onto his jacket sleeve. He looked at my boots, my nervous posture, the way I kept my hands in my pockets like I didn’t know what to do with them.

“First day?” he said.

I nodded.

He stuck out his hand. “Welcome. I’m your steward.”

I didn’t know what that meant yet.

The shift started with a meeting. Not a manager lecture, not a warning about productivity, but a circle of workers talking about the day. Someone mentioned a machine guard that had been loose the day before. Someone else said a new schedule proposal was coming up for a vote. Raul introduced me, and a few people clapped lightly like I’d just joined a team instead of taken a job.

It was strange. I kept waiting for the part where someone told us to hurry up and stop talking.

Later that morning I was helping move a pallet when the forklift operator shut his machine off and waved me over.

“You’re new,” he said. “Nobody showed you the lift zone lines yet, huh?”

I shook my head.

Instead of yelling or telling me to figure it out
